title: Launching the Reservation Homes Project
content:
# We Are Building More Than Homes

It's no secret that reservation communities are in deep need of quality, affordable homes. For too long, our families have lived in substandard housing — overcrowded, underfunded, and overlooked.

With years of home construction efforts under our belts, we are proud to announce the official launch of the **Reservation Homes Project**.

## What This Means for Our Community

This initiative will provide much needed housing for tribal members, offer sustainable income for Indigenous employees, and create lasting value for stakeholders across the region.

By 2035, we envision a reservation where:

- Tribal employees thrive as highly-trained construction industry experts
- Native families enjoy affordable, high-quality homes
- The next generation of construction professionals nurtures and hones their skills right here on our land

## Our Commitment

We are committed to transparency every step of the way. Follow this blog for regular updates on construction progress, community milestones, volunteer opportunities, and stories from the families whose lives we are building together.

*Together, we raise more than tipis — we raise futures.*
excerpt: It's no secret that reservation communities are in deep need of quality, affordable homes. We are proud to announce the launch of the Reservation Homes Project.
author: Tipi Raisers Team

title: Community Survey Results: Understanding Our Housing Need
content:
# What Our Community Told Us

Before breaking ground, we needed to listen. In early 2024, our team conducted a comprehensive housing needs survey across the reservation, reaching over 200 tribal households.

## Key Findings

The results were clear and reinforced what our community has known for generations:

- **67%** of respondents reported living in overcrowded conditions
- **42%** reported significant structural issues in their current homes
- **89%** expressed strong support for the Reservation Homes Project
- **73%** said they or a family member would be interested in construction training

## What's Next

These findings directly shape our build plan. We're prioritizing families with the most urgent needs while ensuring our construction training program gives preference to community members first.

Thank you to everyone who participated. Your voices are the foundation of this project.
excerpt: Over 200 tribal households spoke — and we listened. Here's what our community housing survey revealed and how it's shaping our build plan.
author: Tipi Raisers Team

title: How You Can Get Involved Right Now
content:
# There Are Many Ways to Help

Building a better future for reservation families takes a whole community. Whether you're a skilled tradesperson, a passionate advocate, or someone who simply wants to contribute — there is a place for you in this movement.

## Volunteer Your Skills

We are actively seeking volunteers with skills in:

- **Construction & Trades** — carpenters, electricians, plumbers, laborers
- **Project Management** — help coordinate build schedules and resources
- **Education & Training** — teach or assist in our construction apprenticeship program
- **Marketing & Communications** — help spread the word about our work
- **Grant Writing** — help us secure funding from tribal, state, and federal programs

## Donate

Every dollar goes directly toward materials, training, and the families we serve. Even a small monthly contribution makes a meaningful difference over time.

## Spread the Word

Share our story. Follow us on social media. Tell your friends and colleagues. Awareness is the first step toward change.

[Visit our Get Involved page](/get-involved) to sign up today.
excerpt: From skilled trades to grant writing, there are many ways to support the Reservation Homes Project. Here's how to get started.
author: Tipi Raisers Team

title: Day 1
content: Today was the first day of beginning to work on the Tiny Homes. Alexander Dawson students Birdie Cardinal, Hannah Johnson, Asher Greer, Jack Kennedy, and Charles Lynch cleared the barn in preparation for setting up the workshop. The barn was filled with junk and had a pungent oder. Filled with toxic waste, trash, and leftover building materials the work was unglamorous and hard. Some of the tasks to be completed included clearing out an outside staging area for the lumber, cleaning the junk inside of the barn and delivering it to the dump, and removing a lot of toxic waste. While these are just the first steps, this will be crucial for the project. This barn will act as the main workshop where the walls for the house will be framed. The work was exhausting and left us dirty and tired but it felt good to take the first step towards building a home.
excerpt: The first day was focused on clearing out the barn to act as a workshop for the project.
author: Tipi Raisers Team
